prompt:
The label-editor rewrite needs proper undo. Our current 'undo' is a snapshot of the whole document per keystroke and it eats memory like nothing else; product also wants multi-step operations (paste-with-reformat) to undo as ONE step, which snapshots can't express cleanly. Command pattern time.

Build `undostack.py` with class `UndoStack`. Commands are plain objects with `apply()` and `revert()` methods — the stack never inspects them beyond that.

- `execute(cmd)` runs cmd.apply() immediately and records it. Any execute of new work throws away the entire redo branch — even if that work later gets rolled back; once history diverged, it diverged.
- `undo()` / `redo()` step through history, returning True, or False when there's nothing to do (never raise for an empty stack — the toolbar just calls it). Undo reverts; redo re-applies. LIFO, obviously.
- `can_undo` / `can_redo` booleans and `undo_depth` / `redo_depth` ints.
- Transactions: `begin()` opens one, `commit()` closes it and the commands executed inside become a SINGLE undo unit — undone in reverse execution order, redone in original order. Committing an empty transaction records nothing. `begin()` nests: an inner commit folds its commands into the enclosing transaction; only the outermost commit creates the undo unit.
- `rollback()` reverts every command of the innermost open transaction (reverse order) and discards them — the enclosing transaction (or the stack) is untouched.
- Guard rails, all RuntimeError: commit or rollback with no open transaction; undo or redo while any transaction is open (the document would tear).

Acceptance tests: `python3 test_undostack.py` — they drive a realistic SetCell command against a dict document plus a tracing command that checks exact apply/revert ordering. Tests are read-only. Stdlib only.
